era_context: The mid-14th century continues with the Hundred Years' War in its early phase, the Yuan Dynasty in advanced decline, the Ottoman Empire expanding in Anatolia, and the Black Death pandemic beginning its westward spread from Central Asia. This year falls in the final years before the plague's documented arrival in Europe (October 1346), though the disease is already devastating populations in Asia and the Islamic world.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Islamic world (plague's spread is less thoroughly documented in surviving Western sources)

events.title: Black Death spreads through Central Asia and the Islamic world
events.region: Central Asia, Islamic world, Mediterranean trade routes
events.description: The plague pandemic, originating in Central Asian rodent populations, continues its westward spread through trade routes. By 1345, the disease is documented in the Golden Horde territories, the Caucasus, and the Islamic world (including Egypt and the Levant). Contemporary Islamic sources (including Ibn al-Wardi and later accounts by al-Maqrizi) describe massive mortality, economic disruption, and social upheaval. The plague reaches the Crimean peninsula and Mediterranean trade ports by late 1345 or early 1346. The scale of mortality in Asia and the Islamic world in 1345 is enormous but less precisely documented in surviving Western sources than the European phase of the pandemic.
key_figures: Merchants and traders along the Silk Road
Islamic physicians and scholars documenting the plague

sources.name: Ibn al-Wardi, Khatira (written c. 1348, describes plague in Syria and Egypt)
Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-hunafa (later compilation, contains accounts of 1345 plague)
Paleogenomic evidence from mass graves in Central Asia and the Islamic world
Chinese sources documenting plague in Yuan territories

events.certainty_note: The plague's presence in Central Asia and the Islamic world by 1345 is attested in multiple sources, though precise dating and casualty figures are uncertain. The disease's arrival in the Mediterranean is documented for late 1345 or early 1346.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English naval dominance and raids
events.region: English Channel, Normandy, Kingdom of France
events.description: English forces under Edward III continue to assert naval dominance in the English Channel and conduct raids on French coastal territories. These operations, part of the broader Hundred Years' War, disrupt French commerce and demonstrate English military capability. The English are preparing for the major campaign that will culminate in the Battle of Crécy in August 1346. Contemporary sources note English military preparations and French defensive measures.

sources.name: English royal records and military accounts

events.certainty_note: English military activity in the Channel and raids on France in 1345 are attested in royal records, though specific operations are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces continued internal instability and regional fragmentation
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty under Toghon Temür continues to experience severe internal instability. Regional warlords, Mongol princes, and Han Chinese officials compete for power and resources. Corruption, inflation, and administrative paralysis are widespread. Peasant unrest and bandit movements are increasing. The central government's authority is weakening, and the dynasty's collapse is becoming increasingly evident to contemporary observers. By 1345, the conditions for major rebellion are ripening.
key_figures: Various regional warlords and Mongol princes

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented in official records.

events.title: Ottoman Empire continues expansion in Anatolia under Orhan I
events.description: Orhan I continues to consolidate Ottoman power in northwestern Anatolia. The Ottoman state is expanding its territory, strengthening its military organization, and establishing administrative structures. By 1345, the Ottomans are the dominant power in the region and are beginning to look toward expansion into the Balkans. Contemporary Byzantine sources note Ottoman military strength and territorial expansion.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion under Orhan I is well-attested, though specific events in 1345 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Papal Schism and ecclesiastical politics in Europe
events.description: The Catholic Church is in a period of institutional stress. Pope Clement VI (r. 1342–1352) is dealing with ecclesiastical politics, heresy, and the early stages of the Great Schism (which will formally begin in 1378). In 1345, the Church is also beginning to grapple with the approaching plague pandemic and its theological implications. Clement VI's papacy is marked by political maneuvering, the Avignon papacy's influence, and attempts to maintain ecclesiastical authority amid social upheaval.
events.certainty_note: Clement VI's papacy and ecclesiastical politics in 1345 are well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of the plague's arrival in specific regions in 1345 is uncertain. Some sources place the plague's arrival in the Mediterranean in late 1345, while others date it to early 1346. The extent of plague mortality in Central Asia and the Islamic world in 1345 is difficult to quantify; Islamic sources provide accounts of massive death tolls, but these are often compiled retrospectively and may conflate events from multiple years. The significance of English naval dominance in 1345 is sometimes overstated; French naval power was also considerable, and the Channel was contested rather than firmly under English control. The Ottoman expansion narrative, while generally accurate, can obscure the fact that Ottoman consolidation was gradual and contested by other Anatolian powers.
historiographic_note: The year 1345 is moderately well-documented for Europe and the Mediterranean, with good sources for English-French military activity and papal politics. However, the plague's spread through Central Asia and the Islamic world is less thoroughly documented in surviving Western sources, creating a geographic bias in the historical record. Chinese sources are available but often compiled retrospectively. Ottoman sources are later compilations. The historiography of 1345 is shaped by the dramatic events of 1346 (Crécy, plague arrival in Europe), which may have overshadowed other significant developments. The year 1345 is often treated as a prelude to 1346 rather than as a significant year in its own right.

graph_edges.note: Plague spread through Central Asia and Islamic world in 1345 leads to arrival in Mediterranean in late 1345/early 1346
English military preparations in 1345 culminate in Battle of Crécy in August 1346
